policy_statement: Adopt Complete Communities plans to reverse historical and racist impacts, address displacement, and preserve neighborhoods in communities of color.
mechanism_description: Adopt Complete Communities plans to help address displacement pressures, preserve neighborhoods in communities of color, and support more equitable community resilience outcomes.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance & land-use planning → ↓ displacement / ↑ social cohesion & access to services → ↓ vulnerability & exposure → ↓ climate risk impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: City adopts and implements “Complete Communities” area plans and associated governance decisions (land-use, housing preservation, community investments, participatory planning) aimed at stabilizing residents and preventing displacement in frontline communities.  
(2) Mediating physical/social process: By preserving affordable housing, maintaining social networks, improving access to essential services, and directing investments to underserved neighborhoods, the community’s adaptive capacity increases and sensitivity to disruptions decreases.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced adverse impacts from climate hazards (e.g., heat waves, flooding, storms) through lower vulnerability, improved preparedness/response, and more equitable recovery (resilience outcomes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate risk by lowering vulnerability and displacement-driven exposure, improving equitable capacity to prepare for and recover from hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Planning to prevent displacement and preserve community stability primarily reduces vulnerability and exposure to climate hazards, improving resilience outcomes.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: While such plans can incidentally influence emissions (e.g., via land-use patterns) or resource efficiency, the stated and most generalizable mechanism is equitable resilience/anti-displacement—an adaptation pathway focused on risk reduction rather than energy/material throughput or carbon sinks.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “complete communities” plan were implemented mainly to reduce VMT or building energy use, that would shift the dominant pathway toward Mitigation/Resource Efficiency; however, that is not part of this canonical mechanism as defined (equitable resilience and anti-displacement).  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy sharing this mechanism—complete communities planning explicitly aimed at anti-displacement and equitable resilience—has its primary climate effect through reduced vulnerability and improved adaptive capacity, so Adaptation is the consistent primary category.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary benefits (e.g., mitigation co-benefits from compact development, or nature-based co-benefits from green space) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Plans labeled “Complete Communities” sometimes primarily pursue compact growth, transit access, or emissions reductions; only those explicitly centered on equitable resilience/anti-displacement fit this mechanism’s Adaptation-first classification.
key_indicators: "Strategy 1: Plan for complete communities"; "Adopt Complete Communities plans"; "reverse historical and racist impacts"; "areas experiencing displacement"; "preserve neighborhoods in communities of color"
co_benefits: Civil rights/equity advancement; neighborhood preservation; reduced displacement; improved access to services
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a governance/anti-displacement regulatory approach if the “plans” embed binding zoning/tenant protections, but the provided text only specifies plan adoption (planning-strategy) rather than enforceable requirements.
